findings.title: Truncation logic can still exceed Discord's 4096-character embed description limit
findings.description: `truncate_body` subtracts only the suffix length from `max_len`, then always adds an extra newline in `format!("{}\n{}", ...)`. For long single-paragraph release notes, the returned description becomes `max_len + 1` characters, and the current test explicitly accepts lengths above 4096. Discord rejects embeds whose description exceeds 4096 characters, so `send_message` will fail and no release notification will be delivered for those releases.
findings.suggestion: Reserve space for the newline as well, and assert that the final description length is `<= 4096`. Update the tests to enforce Discord's actual embed limit instead of allowing `4096 + suffix + 1`.

findings.title: Default state file in `/tmp` is vulnerable to symlink and cross-process interference
findings.description: The default `last_tag_file` is a predictable path under `/tmp`. On multi-user systems, another process can pre-create or replace that path with a symlink, and `std::fs::write` will follow it. That lets an untrusted local user influence where the bot writes. Even without an active attacker, multiple bot instances on the same host will contend for the same file and can suppress or duplicate notifications.
findings.suggestion: Store the marker in an application-owned directory with restricted permissions, such as under the bot's config/data directory, and open/update it atomically with appropriate file locking if multiple instances are possible.

findings.title: Release notification state updates are not atomic, so parallel bot instances can double-notify
findings.description: `check_and_notify` does a separate read, notify, and write using plain `read_to_string`/`write`. If two bot processes or replicas share the same `last_tag_file`, both can observe the old tag, both send the Discord message, and then both write the new tag. This is a classic TOCTOU race around the persisted marker.
findings.suggestion: Use an atomic compare-and-swap style update for the persisted tag, or guard the file with an advisory lock before reading and updating it. If the bot can run in multiple replicas, move this state into the existing database instead of a shared temp file.

summary: The release-checker diff has one Discord API-breaking truncation bug and two state-persistence issues around the release marker file, including an unsafe `/tmp` default and a multi-instance race.
